title: Chapter 6: The Purchase of a God
content: The Hollow Cathedral looked different in Roxanne's second visit.

She could see the prices now. Every surface, every chain, every symbol — they all carried price tags. The crystal pillars were worth 20,000 Obols each. The obsidian floor was 150,000. The chains binding Silas flickered with their buyout number: 100,000,000 Obols.
The Bound God himself had a price floating above his head — not for sale, but for valuation:

*SILAS, THE BOUND GOD*
*— Classification: Primordial, First Order*
*— Estimated Market Value: UNPRICED (beyond current Nether-Grid transaction range)*
*— Current Status: SEALED (contract held by Primordial Collective)*
"Back so soon?"
His voice echoed through the chamber. He hadn't opened his eyes yet. But the chains shifted slightly, as if he was leaning forward.
"I told you I'd think about it."
"And you've thought."
"I've thought."
Silas opened his eyes. Those ancient red irises fixed on her with an intensity that made the air feel heavier.
"And what conclusion has the heir reached?"
Roxanne walked forward. Stopped ten feet from the cage of chains. The golden symbols flared as she approached, reacting to the Primordial card in her pocket.
"I'm going to buy you."
A pause. Then Silas smiled — a slow, genuine expression that transformed his angular features into something almost warm.
"I know."
"You knew before I walked in."
"I knew when you left the first time. You had the look of someone who'd made a decision and needed time to accept it."
"That's annoyingly perceptive."
"Four billion years of observation. You learn to read people."
Roxanne pulled out the black card.
"One hundred million Obols. That's the buyout, right?"
"That's the seal's price. The Null-Iron Chains. Buy the chains, and I'm free."
"And then?"
"And then I serve you. For as long as you hold my contract. Absolute loyalty. Absolute protection. Absolute obedience."
"And if I don't want a servant?"
"Then you have a god as a friend. Which is still considerably better than having a god as an enemy."
Roxanne laughed.
"You really do have good timing for someone who's been alone for four billion years."
"I've had a lot of time to practice."
She lifted the card.
"Purchase: Null-Iron Chains — complete buyout. Price: 100,000,000 Obols."
The card pulsed. Once. Twice. Then it grew warm — not the gentle warmth of approval, but a deep, resonant heat that spread up her arm and into her chest.
*PURCHASE APPROVED. 100,000,000 OBOLS DEBITED.*
*NULL-IRON CHAINS: DISSOLVING.*
The golden symbols on the chains flickered. Dimmed. Went dark.
And then the chains shattered.
The sound was deafening. A million shards of crystallized light exploding outward, ricocheting off the obsidian floor, embedding in the crystal pillars. Roxanne threw up her arm to shield her face.
When she looked again, Silas was standing.

He was taller than she'd realized — six-four, maybe six-five. The shadows that had clung to him in chains now flowed freely around his form, shaping themselves into a dark Victorian-style coat. His silver hair drifted as if underwater. His red eyes glowed in the dim cathedral light.
He looked at his hands. Flexed his fingers. Rolled his shoulders.
"Ah."
It was a single syllable, but it carried four billion years of weight.
"That's better."
He turned to Roxanne. And then — to her complete surprise — he dropped to one knee.
"Roxanne Vance. Heir of the Primordial Void. Holder of my contract."
His voice was formal now. Ceremonial.
"I am Silas, the Bound God, last of the First Order Primordials. By the laws of the Nether-Grid, my service is yours. My loyalty is yours. My power answers to your will."
He looked up. Met her eyes.
"What would you have me do?"
Roxanne stood there, in a cathedral beneath her art school, facing a god who had just knelt to her.
She thought about it for exactly three seconds.
"First," she said, "I want a coffee. The machine in the dean's office is good but I can't work it. Can you make coffee?"
Silas blinked.
"I have unmake reality," he said slowly. "And you want me to make coffee?"
"I want you to make a latte. With oat milk. And a decent foam-to-espresso ratio."
He stared at her.
Then he laughed. A genuine, surprised laugh that echoed through the cathedral.
"Four billion years," he said, "and I did not see that coming."
He stood. Adjusted his shadow-coat.
"Let's find this coffee machine."

*End of Chapter 6*
\n---\n
Silas caught them.

The chains shattered into golden fragments that hung in the air like frozen fireworks before dissolving into nothing. And then he was moving — not walking, but flowing — catching Roxanne before she hit the ground.
"I've got you."
She looked up at him. His face was inches from hers. His eyes were no longer the dull red of a prisoner. They glowed.
"You're heavy," she said.
"I'm a Primordial god. We have high bone density."
"That's not a real thing."
"It is now. I just made it up."
He set her down gently. She steadied herself, then walked past him to examine the space where the chains had been.
The floating price tags around him had changed.
Before: *SILAS — BOUND GOD — SEALED — 100,000,000 OBOLS (BUYOUT).*
After: *SILAS — BOUND GOD — STATUS: ACTIVE — CONTRACT HOLDER: ROXANNE VANCE — NOT FOR SALE.*
"Not for sale?" she asked.
"I updated my status. I'm no longer available on the open market."
"You can do that?"
"I'm a god. I can update my own listing. The Exchange respects self-classification."
She stared at him.
"You're telling me that for four billion years, you could have just changed your listing to 'not for sale' and walked out?"
"The chains were real. The listing was just a listing. I couldn't move until the chains were bought."
"So the whole time, you were trapped by a piece of paperwork?"
"All contracts are paperwork. The chains were just the physical manifestation of the contract. You bought the contract. The chains dissolved. Simple."
She rubbed her temples.
"I'm going to need to understand this economy better."
"You have time. The Nether-Grid is patient."
She looked at him. At his shadow-coat. At his silver hair. At the faint glow in his red eyes.
"Okay. Let's go see what my new campus looks like."
The walk back through the Hollow Cathedral was different without the chains.

The violet-black fog that had hung in the upper reaches was thinner now. The crystal pillars no longer hummed with the weight of the seal. The obsidian floor reflected Roxanne's footsteps clearly, no longer clouded by centuries of accumulated shadow.
"The Cathedral is changing," she said.
"The seal's dissolution is affecting the local reality matrix," Silas said. "This space was warped to contain me. Now that I'm no longer contained, the space is... relaxing."
"Relaxing."
"Returning to its natural state. Give it a few weeks and this will just be an unusually large basement."
They reached the iron door at the top of the stairs. Roxanne pushed it open and stepped out into the Fine Arts building's basement corridor. The familiar smell of turpentine and paper greeted her.
Home.
"What now?" Silas asked.
"Now I figure out how to run a university during the apocalypse. I need a command center. I need a communication system. I need to find out how many survivors are on this campus and what they need."
"That's a lot."
"It's called administration. I minored in it."
"You minored in administration at an art school?"
"I minored in arts administration. Specifically. I was going to run a gallery. Turns out the skills transfer."
She started walking toward the stairs.
"First things first: I need coffee. Then I need a map. Then I need to meet my new tenants."
